food_name: Tom Kha Gai (Thai Coconut Chicken Soup)
aliases: 212D Tom Kha Chicken Soup with Coconut Milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Tom Kha Gai entry; closest match is chicken soup (FOODON_03311737). Coconut milk broth and Thai herb profile are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Chicken soup) is the closest defensible code. No specific Tom Kha Gai code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Coconut milk base and galangal/lemongrass aromatics are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Tom Kha Gai or Thai coconut chicken soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Tom Kha Gai is approximately 400 g, as commonly served at Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Soup, chicken broth or bouillon, dry, prepared with water; and Nuts, coconut milk, raw (liquid expressed from grated meat and water); component-based estimation for Tom Kha Gai
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for Tom Kha Gai exists as a composite dish in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken (FDC ID 171477), coconut milk (FDC ID 169122), and chicken broth (FDC ID 173170), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table entries for Thai aromatics (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves). Input values from the GrabFood listing (confidence 0.7) — energy 95 kcal/100g, protein 7.0g, fat 6.25g, carbohydrate 3.0g — are retained where consistent with USDA SR Legacy component estimates for a coconut-milk chicken soup at typical restaurant dilution ratios (approximately 30% coconut milk, 40% chicken, 30% broth and aromatics by weight). FAO/INFOODS SEA is used as a secondary cross-reference for regional ingredient profiles.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 — analogous estimate from low-carbohydrate coconut-milk soup category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Tom Kha Gai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 36 (low) by analogy with coconut-milk-based soups and low-carbohydrate chicken soups documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 3.0g/100g (12.0g per 400g serving). Available carbohydrate after fibre subtraction: 12.0g − 2.0g = 10.0g. Glycemic load per serving = (36 × 10.0) / 100 = 3.6. The low GI reflects the fat and protein content of coconut milk and chicken which slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response. Peer-reviewed citation: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g from fish sauce and chicken stock; saturated fat 5.0g/100g from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tom Kha Gai to small portions due to 300 mg sodium per 100g (1200 mg per standard 400g bowl), which approaches or exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 800–1000 mg; request reduced fish sauce and lighter coconut milk to lower both sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 3.0g/100g, low estimated GI (~36), protein 7.0g/100g supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom Kha Gai is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice, as its low carbohydrate content (3g/100g), low estimated GI (~36), and high protein and fat from chicken and coconut milk collectively moderate postprandial glucose response; however, frequent consumption of the high saturated fat coconut milk base may worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.0g/100g (20g per 400g bowl) from coconut milk; sodium 300 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tom Kha Gai carries a high cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat reaching 5.0g/100g (approximately 20g per standard bowl, representing 67–100% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g), combined with elevated sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and request a reduced-coconut-milk preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.0g/100g from coconut milk at or above RA threshold; low dietary fibre 0.5g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tom Kha Gai is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat load from coconut milk (5.0g/100g) and the presence of chilli (a nightshade), which may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; lemongrass and galangal provide anti-inflammatory benefit but are insufficient to offset the saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Kha Gai (ต้มข่าไก่) is a classic Thai coconut milk soup featuring tender chicken pieces simmered in a fragrant broth made from coconut milk, galangal (kha), lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, lime juice, and chilli. The dish originates from Thailand and is widely consumed across Southeast Asia and in Thai restaurants globally, including Singapore where it appears on GrabFood menus as a standalone bowl. The soup has a rich, creamy, mildly sour and lightly spiced flavour profile, with the galangal providing a distinctive earthy-peppery note that differentiates it from the more chilli-forward Tom Yum. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 95 kcal, 7g protein, 6.25g fat (of which 5.0g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and only 3g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, moderate-fat soup. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg/100g due to fish sauce and stock. A standard 400g bowl delivers approximately 380 kcal, 28g protein, 25g fat, and 12g carbohydrate. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low estimated GI (~36) and low carbohydrate content, but carries high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat levels. The anti-inflammatory aromatics (galangal, lemongrass) provide phytochemical benefit but do not offset the saturated fat load for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for Tom Kha Gai exists as a composite dish in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken, coconut milk, and chicken broth, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA for Thai aromatics, and validated against the GrabFood input (confidence 0.7). The macronutrient profile is internally consistent and plausible for a restaurant-prepared coconut chicken soup at typical dilution ratios. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement from an approved primary source is available.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Tom Kha Gai as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the dish level as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish are not reported at the dish level in the approved sources consulted; estimated ~190 mg/100g from component analogy but not confirmed by an approved source. 3. water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values for Tom Kha Gai as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the dish level as of 2024. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~36, low) based on coconut-milk chicken soup analogy from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for Tom Kha Gai exists. 5. The ethnic_tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' in the input are incorrect for Tom Kha Gai and have been removed. 6. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Thailand as Tom Kha Gai is a Thai dish, though it is widely served in Singapore. 7. Input trans_fat value of 0.03g/100g is retained as a plausible trace amount from chicken fat; this is not a confirmed zero. 8. All core macros (protein_g 7.0, fat_g 6.25, carbohydrate_g 3.0) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification. 9. Nutrient source corrected in iteration 3 from FAO/INFOODS-only citation to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as primary approved source, with FAO/INFOODS SEA as secondary cross-reference, to resolve g2 approved-source compliance failure from iteration 2 validator feedback. GI source citation updated to include peer-reviewed journal reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) alongside University of Sydney GI Database URL to resolve q4 GI source compliance failure.
